name: Metamaterial Mechanisms
short_description: 3D-printed objects whose internal cellular microstructure makes the object itself a working machine, with mechanical function encoded directly in geometry.
long_description: Metamaterial Mechanisms, presented by Alexandra Ion and colleagues (including Patrick Baudisch) at UIST 2016, reconceives metamaterials as machines rather than passive materials. By arranging 3D-printed cellular grids that include specialized shearing cells, a single printed block can convert input motion into controlled macroscopic movement, demonstrated through examples such as a door latch that turns rotary handle motion into linear latch motion and a Jansen-style walker. It matters to physical AI and interaction design as a foundational tangible-fabrication technique: behavior lives in the structure and material itself rather than in motors, electronics, or computation, expanding the vocabulary of programmable matter and embodied interaction.
ai_note: Function is encoded purely in the printed cellular geometry; there is no AI, ML, electronics, or computation running inside the object.

images.caption: Teaser figure showing a 3D-printed metamaterial door latch whose cellular microstructure converts rotary handle motion into linear latch motion.
images.alt_text: A white 3D-printed block made of a fine grid of cells forming a door latch mechanism, with arrows indicating rotational input motion converting to linear output motion.

sources.title: Metamaterial Mechanisms
sources.publisher: Interactive Structures Lab, Carnegie Mellon University

interactions.title: Turn a handle, throw the latch — no hinges, no screws
interactions.description: Press down on a one-piece 3D-printed door latch and its internal grid of cells shears, converting the rotary motion of the handle into the straight-line travel that retracts the bolt. The whole machine is a single printed block with no assembled moving parts.

interactions.title: A block of cells that walks
interactions.description: Drive a single printed block patterned as a Jansen linkage and its coordinated cells flex in sequence so the whole object steps forward like a walking machine, with the gait encoded entirely in the microstructure rather than in any motor or joint.

interactions.title: One special cell that does all the work: the shear cell
interactions.description: Designers compose mechanisms by placing a specialized cell whose only ability is to shear; rigid cells hold shape while shear cells let force route through the block, so pushing on one face produces controlled, predictable movement somewhere else in the solid.

interactions.title: Draw a machine in a 3D cell editor, then print it
interactions.description: Instead of CAD-modeling gears and linkages, a maker opens the team's custom 3D editor, paints rigid and shear cells into a grid to lay out the motion they want, and sends the single block straight to a printer with the mechanism already built in.

interactions.title: Dial a code into a block of plastic to unlock a door
interactions.description: A follow-on extends the technique into a combination door lock printed as one block: a person turns dials to enter a numeric code, the cell structure itself checks the code, and only a correct entry mechanically releases the latch — computation done purely in geometry.

interactions.title: Re-grounding the technique for engineers and editors
interactions.description: A later CHI 2019 paper formalizes how these cell mechanisms deform and constrains them so a tool can simulate and verify a design before printing, turning the original demos into a reusable design method others can build on.

interactions.title: Assembly-free assistive grips
interactions.description: Print a one-piece jar opener or pull-tab tool whose shearing cells amplify a weak squeeze into a stronger pinch at the tip — a compliant gripper with no pins or springs to wear out or trap fingers, sized to an individual hand.

interactions.title: Tamper-evident shipping seals
interactions.description: Embed a metamaterial latch in a printed enclosure so it can only be opened by a specific motion or key shape; the structure stays sealed under any other push or pull, giving a mechanically encoded, electronics-free closure for sensitive packages.
